title: GitLab projects, groups & hierarchy
topic: GitLab projects, groups, organisations, epics, issues, hierarchy, and visibility — for portfolio product design
summary: GitLab organises work in a 5-tier container hierarchy (Organization > Group > Subgroup > Project > WorkItem) with Epics living at group level and Issues at project level, unified under a single WorkItem storage model built on the issues table.
content:
Contents
  1. Summary
  2. Key Findings
  3. Detailed Analysis
  4. Data Tables
  5. Sources
  6. Gaps and Limitations

Summary

GitLab structures work across five nested containers: Organization (experimental, above groups) → Group (strategy layer, hosts Epics) → Subgroup (up to 20 levels deep) → Project (execution unit, hosts Issues/MRs/CI) → WorkItem (Epic, Issue, Task — all stored on the unified issues table). Epics are Premium/Ultimate only; multi-level child Epics require Ultimate. Visibility cascades parent-to-child and can only be equal-or-more-restrictive at each level.

Key Findings

  • Work item hierarchy: Epic (group) → Child Epic (Ultimate only, 7 levels max) → Issue (project) → Task (all tiers). Maximum 5,000 direct children per parent.
  • Unified WorkItem storage: all work item types (Epic, Issue, Task, Incident, OKR) are rows in the issues table via Single Table Inheritance; issue_type column distinguishes type.
  • Parent-child stored via a work_item_parent_links link table (not a direct FK column), enforcing single-parent constraint; multi-parent is not supported.
  • Subgroups nest up to 20 levels deep; projects live at any level.
  • Organization (above top-level groups) is still experimental (feature flag organization_switching since GitLab 18.4) — not production-ready.
  • Epics live at group level; Issues live at project level. Cross-hierarchy issues can be added to an Epic via full URL paste only.
  • Visibility levels: Private, Internal (self-managed/dedicated only), Public. Child visibility must be ≤ parent visibility.
  • Roles: Guest, Planner, Reporter, Security Manager, Developer, Maintainer, Owner (7 total as of recent releases). Inherited down the group/subgroup/project chain.

Detailed Analysis

Q1: Work item hierarchy and edition gating

The canonical hierarchy is: Epic (group-scoped) → Child Epic → (up to 7 levels total) → Issue (project-scoped) → Task (issue-scoped). Flat issues and tasks are available on all tiers (Free, Premium, Ultimate). Epics require Premium or Ultimate. Multi-level (nested) child Epics require Ultimate. The roadmap Gantt view requires Premium or Ultimate. The total nesting depth of epics alone is documented as up to 7 levels. Source: Epics docs, Child items docs.

Q2: Storage model — unified WorkItem

GitLab migrated Epics, Issues, Tasks, Incidents, Objectives, Key Results, and Test Cases into a single unified WorkItem model backed by the issues database table (Single Table Inheritance). The WorkItems::WorkItem class sets self.table_name = 'issues'; the legacy Issue class inherits from it. The issue_type column distinguishes types; a separate work_item_types table holds type metadata. The migration completed across GitLab 17.2–18.1, with GitLab 18.10 replacing the old /epics/:iid and /issues/:iid UI with a unified Plan > Work items view. Source: Work items development docs.

Q3: Parent-child mechanics

Parent-child relationships are stored in a dedicated link table (work_item_parent_links), not as a direct FK column on the issues row. This is confirmed by the GitLab issue tracker reference “Add parent-child work item table (#362125)” and the single-parent enforcement described in public docs. Multi-parent is explicitly not supported — adding a child to a new parent automatically unlinks it from its current parent. Cycle prevention is handled by the hierarchy widget's type-level restriction rules (defined in Ruby definition classes under app/models/work_items/types_framework/), which restrict which types can be parents or children of a given type. Source: Child items docs, Work items dev docs.

Q4: Rollups

The Epic child-items section header displays: (a) count of descendant work items, (b) total weight (summing all child issues and tasks), and (c) progress percentage (completed vs total child items by count and weight). Task weights are included in parent issue weight. On the Roadmap view, each Epic bar shows title, progress, and completed weight percentage; hovering reveals start/due dates and weight detail. Burndown and burnup charts exist for Milestones (project and group level) and Iterations; Epics themselves do not have a dedicated burndown chart page — they use the roadmap progress bar instead. Source: Roadmap docs, Milestones docs.

Q5: Reparenting

Issues can be moved between Epics by editing the issue's parent field or via the Epic's child-items UI (drag-and-drop supported). Child Epics can be reparented to different parent Epics. Moving a parent Issue to a different project also moves all its child Tasks. The single-parent constraint means reparenting automatically removes the old link. Moving issues between Epics requires Ultimate tier. Source: Child items docs.

Q6: Subtree filtering UX

The Roadmap (Gantt chart) shows Epics in a group, its subgroups, or a project. Users can expand Epic rows to reveal child Epics inline. From any Epic detail page, a “View roadmap filtered to this epic's descendants” action scopes the roadmap to that subtree. Additional filters: Author, Label, Milestone, Confidentiality, Epic, Your Reaction, Groups. The Epic tree view inside an Epic shows the full child/grandchild hierarchy expandably. Source: Roadmap docs.

Q7: Project definition

A GitLab Project is a broad container for development work and is the primary unit where code, CI/CD, and issue tracking reside. It contains: a Git repository (files, branches, history), Issues and Merge Requests, CI/CD pipelines, wikis and documentation, packages, dashboards, and integrations. “Most work in GitLab is done in a project.” Projects are not peers of groups — they are children of groups or personal namespaces. Source: Working with projects docs.

Q8: Group hierarchy and subgroup nesting

Groups are the organisational unit above Projects. They form a tree: Top-level Group → Parent Group → Subgroup → ... → Project. Subgroups can be nested up to 20 levels deep. Projects can exist at any level of the group tree. A group can contain any number of subgroups and projects. Source: Subgroups docs.

Q9: Organization — current state

The Organization concept sits above top-level Groups as the highest administrative tier. It is currently in experimental/development stage — not production-ready. Introduced in GitLab 16.1 with feature flag ui_for_organizations (disabled by default); feature flag renamed to organization_switching in GitLab 18.4. Available for testing only. Features: define settings across all groups/subgroups/projects, aggregate data, switch between organizations via sidebar dropdown. Development tracked at epic 9265. Source: Organization docs.

Q10: What lives at each level

Level-by-level breakdown of primary work items and management objects:

LevelPrimary Work ItemsManagement Objects
OrganizationNone (experimental admin layer)Settings aggregation, org-switching
Top-level GroupEpics, OKRsSecurity Dashboard, Compliance Center, Value Stream Analytics, Group Milestones, Iterations, Roadmap
Subgroup (any depth)Epics (inherited from group concept)Group Milestones, Iterations, Roadmap (scoped to subgroup)
ProjectIssues, Tasks, Merge Requests, IncidentsProject Milestones, CI/CD pipelines, Branches, Releases, Packages, Wiki

Epics are definitively group-scoped; Issues are project-scoped. Source: Epics docs, Issues docs.

Q11: Cross-project Epics

A group-level Epic can contain Issues from any Project within its group hierarchy (group or descendant subgroups). New issues can only be created from an Epic in projects that belong to the Epic's group or descendant subgroups. However, existing issues from completely different group hierarchies can be added to an Epic by pasting the full issue URL — they will not appear in search results within the Epic's scope but can be linked this way. Source: Epics docs, search results confirming GA in GitLab 17.6.

Q12: Visibility levels and cascade

Three visibility levels exist:

  • Private: Only explicit members can access. Private groups may only contain Private subgroups and projects.
  • Internal: Any authenticated user (excluding external users) can access. Available on self-managed and GitLab Dedicated only (not GitLab.com for new groups). Internal groups may contain Internal or Private subgroups/projects.
  • Public: Anyone including unauthenticated users can access. Public groups may contain Public, Internal, or Private subgroups/projects.

Cascade rule: “The visibility setting for a project must be at least as restrictive as the visibility of its parent group.” You cannot create a Public project inside a Private group. When lowering a group's visibility, all children must already be at least as restrictive. Source: Public access docs.

Q13: Permissions and roles

GitLab has 7 default roles (as of recent releases), listed in ascending permission order:

RoleKey CapabilitiesNotable Restrictions
GuestView and comment on Issues and EpicsCannot clone private/internal repos
PlannerCreate/manage Issues, Epics, Milestones, IterationsCannot push code
ReporterView code, create Issues, clone reposCannot push code or manage protected branches
Security ManagerView/manage vulnerabilities, compliance, audit eventsNo code push access
DeveloperPush to unprotected branches, create MRsCannot manage project settings
MaintainerManage branches, MRs, CI/CD settings, project membersCannot delete project
OwnerFull control including deletion and visibility settings—

Roles are assigned at both group and project level. A Minimal Access role (Premium/Ultimate) allows granting group visibility without automatic project access. Source: Permissions docs.

Q14: Project access via Groups — inheritance

Yes, permissions are fully inherited. Members added to a parent group automatically gain membership and the same role in all descendant subgroups and projects. The system supports four membership sources: (1) Direct (explicitly added to the project), (2) Inherited (from parent group), (3) Shared (via group-to-group sharing), (4) Indirect (combination). You cannot assign a user a role in a subgroup that is lower than their role in a parent group. A user always retains the highest role across all memberships. Source: Subgroups docs, Permissions docs.

Q15: Strategy at Group, execution at Project

This characterisation is accurate and supported by GitLab's own documentation and blog posts. Groups are the strategy/programme layer: they host Epics, Roadmaps, Milestones (group-wide), Iterations, Value Stream Analytics, Security Dashboard, and Compliance Center. Projects are the execution layer: they host the Git repo, Issues, Merge Requests, CI/CD pipelines, project-level Milestones, and Releases. GitLab's recommended enterprise hierarchy model uses top-level groups for programme management and child projects for team-level delivery, with cross-project Epics tying together work from multiple project teams. Source: GitLab organisational hierarchy best practices blog, Groups docs.

Visibility Compatibility Matrix

Parent VisibilityAllowed Child Visibility
PublicPublic, Internal, Private

Gaps and Limitations

  • work_item_parent_links table schema: The exact column schema (FKs, constraints) for this table is not in public user-facing docs; only inferred from GitLab issue #362125 title and behavioural docs. Source code review would be required for exact DDL.
  • Cycle prevention algorithm: The mechanism (Ruby-level type restriction vs DB-level constraint) is not publicly documented in detail. Known only to use in-memory Ruby definition classes per type.
  • Epic burndown charts: The dedicated epic burndown/burnup chart docs page redirected to a GitLab auth wall during research. Progress rollup detail was sourced from search results and Roadmap/milestone docs instead.
  • Organization feature completeness: The Organization layer is still experimental; its final scope, billing model, and API surface are not yet documented for production use.
  • Internal visibility on GitLab.com: Documentation states Internal is available on self-managed and Dedicated — its current availability for new projects on GitLab.com was not explicitly confirmed and may have restrictions.
  • Custom work item types: Planned for Q1 FY27 per development docs; not yet available. Custom types may alter the hierarchy model when released.
